real_meaning: ～ておく expresses doing something <ruby>ahead<rt></rt></ruby> of time as preparation or precaution for a future situation — the feeling is 'I'll take care of this now so it's ready/handled later.' It can also mean leaving something in a certain state intentionally, implying 'I'll let it stay that way for now (for a reason).' The key nuance is deliberate forethought: you're not just doing something, you're setting the stage.

formation.rule: Attach ておく (or its contracted spoken form とく) to the て-form of a verb. Example: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べる → <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べておく (written/standard) / <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べとく (casual spoken). <ruby>予約<rt>よやく</rt></ruby>する → <ruby>予約<rt>よやく</rt></ruby>しておく. い-adjectives and nouns do not attach directly — only verbs use this pattern.
formation.common_mistake: Learners sometimes attach おく directly to the dictionary form instead of the て-form. Wrong: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるおく ✗. Correct: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べておく ✓.

sentences.jp: <ruby>明日<rt>あした</rt></ruby>のパーティーのために、<ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>み<ruby>物<rt>もの</rt></ruby>を<ruby>買<rt>か</rt></ruby>っとくね。
sentences.translation: I'll go ahead and buy drinks for tomorrow's party.
sentences.notes: とく is the contracted casual form of ておく, very natural in everyday spoken Japanese among friends.

sentences.jp: <ruby>旅行<rt>りょこう</rt></ruby>の<ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>に、ホテルを<ruby>予約<rt>よやく</rt></ruby>しておきました。
sentences.translation: I made a hotel reservation in advance before the trip.
sentences.notes: This is the classic preparatory use — the booking was done deliberately so that things would be sorted later.

sentences.jp: <ruby>会議<rt>かいぎ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>資料<rt>しりょう</rt></ruby>は、あらかじめ<ruby>全員<rt>ぜんいん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>配布<rt>はいふ</rt></ruby>しておくべきです。
sentences.translation: The meeting materials should be distributed to all participants in advance.
sentences.notes: Combining ておく with べき adds a prescriptive tone — this is what good preparation requires.

sentences.jp: それについては、<ruby>今<rt>いま</rt></ruby>は<ruby>何<rt>なに</rt></ruby>も<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>わないでおきます。
sentences.translation: For now, I'll refrain from saying anything about that.
sentences.notes: Surprising to learners: ておく can be used with a negative te-form (ないでおく) to mean intentionally leaving something undone or unsaid — a deliberate non-action.

confused_with.contrast: ～ておく is about deliberate preparation — you do something intentionally to set things up for the future. ～てしまう, by contrast, expresses completion with an emotional edge — either regret ('I went and did it...') or relief at finishing something fully. Compare: <ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>をやっておいた means 'I did the homework ahead of time (so I'm prepared),' focusing on readiness. <ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>をやってしまった means 'I went ahead and finished the homework (it's done/off my plate),' focusing on the fact of completion, sometimes with a sense of relief or even mild regret. If you're thinking about a future situation you're preparing for, use ておく; if you're commenting on the fact that something is now fully done, use てしまう.

bunpro_tip: Picture yourself as a <ruby>準備<rt>じゅんび</rt></ruby>のいい人 (a well-prepared person) setting dominoes in place before they need to fall — that forward-thinking 'I'll handle it now for later' feeling IS ておく. When you see とく in casual speech, train yourself to immediately recognize it as the spoken contraction so it doesn't trip you up on BunPro's listening-style prompts.
